Designing elegant PDF from markdown

I need to design an elegant PDF from a markdown file, capturing aesthetics from some screenshots. There are four images to guide the design, not three like user mentioned. The goal is a narrow column, small fonts with a balanced serif and sans-serif style. The page should have an off-white background, elegant headings, and some subtle ornamentation. I’ll need to carefully parse the markdown and ensure the structure reflects the visual references.

Choosing PDF layout details

I’m thinking about the PDF layout. A4 seems best, as it matches the visual references better than US Letter. The page should have lots of whitespace with a narrow center column at 55-60% width, and a light warm grey background (#f4f1ea). For the content area, I'll use a similar light beige background with a centered, slightly lighter panel for the text. Shadows are tricky in a PDF, but I can use a rectangle border for effect.

Choosing fonts and borders for the PDF

I’m thinking about the borders and fonts. The page should have a light warm grey background with a white column enclosed by a 1pt border in a slightly darker beige. For fonts, I could use built-in ones like Times-Roman (serif) and Helvetica (sans). While I can register other fonts, I’ll stick to built-ins for availability to meet the user’s balance of elegance.

Choosing the font source and execution method

I think it’s better to embed open-source fonts from local sources, checking if there are any in the /home/oai/share directory. The environment requires using the container tool for code execution, and I must stick to that instead of using Python directly. I’ll review the instructions from /home/oai/skills/pdfs/skill.md to confirm.

Designing the PDF layout and formatting

I’m focusing on the design of the PDF layout, thinking A4 size works well. The central column is around 360 pt, with full-page background. I’ll use a baseline grid for line spacing and select fonts based on user preference. Serif for headings, sans for body text, with mono for URLs and code. The title will be bold, large serif, and I'll include subtitles, section headings, and body text with balanced fonts as specified. Will ensure the layout is neat and elegant.

Handling tables, blockquotes, and design elements

We need to handle tables in the PDF, adjusting them within a narrow column with small sans fonts, light borders, and alternating row backgrounds. For blockquotes, I’ll use serif italics with a left border line. I’ll add small ornamental symbols at section breaks, and ensure proper page headers, footers, and document title alignment. I'll use a light warm background and slightly tinted column card background, ensuring citations are included where needed.

Preparing PDF workflow

I need to check the skill.md file for the proper workflow, using container to open it (since that's how I should run code). After inspecting, I will save the generated PDF in the /mnt/data directory and provide the user with a download link. I'll make sure the process is smooth for them.

Defining custom styles for document

I’ll register the fonts in ReportLab and set Roboto-Regular as the default font for the body text. For specific styles:

  • Title: Garamond12-Regular, 24 pt, center aligned, with spaceAfter.

  • Subtitle/meta: Roboto-Regular, 9 pt.

  • H2, H3: Garamond-Bold/Regular, with leading, spaceBefore/After for structure.

Body formatting done through tags like <b> and <i>.

Handling bold fonts in ReportLab

For TrueType fonts, ReportLab doesn’t automatically map the <b> tag to the bold font unless I define a bold version. To handle this properly, I'll use registerFontFamily to register Roboto with normal, bold, italic, and boldItalic versions. Then, referencing the 'Roboto' family will auto-switch the <b> tag to its bold version.

Font family registration and styling

For clarity, I'll register fonts with specific names like 'Roboto-Regular', 'Roboto-Bold', etc. Then, I’ll register a font family 'Roboto' for mapping. By setting style.fontName to 'Roboto', the <b> tag will automatically refer to the correct bold version if early mappings are established. I'll follow this with Garamond for headings, ensuring <b> applies bold appropriately.

Testing blockquote styling

For blockquotes, I'll try Garamond-Italic 9 or Roboto-Italic 9 with a leading of 12, left indent 18, and text color #333. Since a typical Paragraph doesn’t support a left border, I’ll create a custom flowable for that. For multiple paragraphs or bullet lists, consider using a Table or a grey box for larger content.

Simplifying blockquote and list parsing

For blockquotes, I can convert the content into Paragraphs with a left indent and add a darker left border. Since Paragraph's style supports border properties, I can use a Table with nested Paragraphs to handle multi-paragraph blockquotes, boxing them neatly. For lists, I'll use ListFlowable with standard bullet styles and handle links accordingly.

Deciding on handling links and text styles

Since Paragraph handles links, I can style them with an underline and color, using #2a5db0 for elegance or keep them black with an underline. Reportlab uses <a href> for links, so I'll convert <a> tags into <link> tags and handle special characters. I'll also consider transforming HTML or Markdown to ReportLab while preserving table content as Paragraph with appropriate TableCell styling.

Then, I’ll set up these styles in the script to maintain consistency throughout the document.

Defining styles

Let's set up the document’s styles: We’ll use 'Roboto' for body text and 'Garamond' for titles, with specific sizes and alignments. We'll account for headers (H2, H3, and H4), a table header style, and blockquotes. For inline markup, we'll need to convert tags like <strong> to <b>. Important note: the platform might not fully support <strong>. We'll handle inline code as needed.

Handling bullet lists and HTML parsing

For bullet lists, I’ll use ListFlowable. While it's possible to create lists with custom bullet graphics, I’m considering using simpler Paragraph elements with bulletText, which will reduce complexity and avoid breaking things. For nested lists, I’ll leave them for now, focusing on handling HTML elements like <h1>, <h2>, and text processing properly. I'll parse the html, skipping unnecessary whitespace. We'll build this out step by step.

Handling HTML conversion functions

When parsing the HTML, I'll differentiate how to handle each tag. For example, the <h3> tag will get styled with style_h3; <ul> and <ol> will be treated as lists, and <blockquote> will be handled as callout boxes. I'll write a function to convert strong to b, em to i, and so on, while ensuring the markup remains compatible with the reportlab PDF generation system.

Handling blockquotes and tables with tailored styles

For blockquotes, I'll parse each nested element and apply the blockquote style. Next, for tables, I’ll calculate column widths based on text length and available width, ensuring each row has equal length. I’ll adjust table styles for appearance, like alternating row colors, padding, and enabling header row repeats across pages.

Designing ornamental dividers and cover page layout

For horizontal dividers, I’ll create a custom flowable that draws a line, with possible triangle accents or text ornaments (like "⟡" or "»»»"). I might use small arrows or draw triangles within a flowable to customize the divider. Also, I'll implement a page break after the cover page for better formatting.

Page and formatting enhancements

I'll detect the first <hr> after the initial title and content and use it as a page break. After that, <hr> can be treated as a divider ornament. Also, I'll ensure that headings are properly styled using Garamond for titles and Roboto for body text, keeping paragraphs from getting too wide.

Formatting adjustments and implementation plan

I’ll manage whitespace by adjusting spaceBefore for headings and blockquotes. For code and footnotes, I’ll use a monospace font like DejaVu Sans Mono. Checkboxes will stay as is, but I may convert [x] to "✓". For ordered lists, I’ll handle the numbering and nested lists with appropriate formatting. Time to create the script!

Rendering markdown as polished PDF
python
bash -lc apply_patch<<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: render_context_pdf.py
+"""Render a markdown brief into a styled, narrow-column PDF.
+
+Design goal: warm paper background, generous whitespace, serif headings + sans body,
+and clean tables/callouts.
+
+Input:  /mnt/data/260117-context-v6.md
+Output: /mnt/data/260117-context-v6.pdf
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import re
+from dataclasses import dataclass
+from pathlib import Path
+from typing import Iterable, List, Optional, Sequence, Tuple
+
+import markdown2
+from bs4 import BeautifulSoup, NavigableString, Tag
+
+from reportlab.lib import colors
+from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import A4
+from reportlab.lib.units import inch
+from reportlab.pdfbase import pdfmetrics
+from reportlab.pdfbase.ttfonts import TTFont
+from reportlab.pdfbase.pdfmetrics import registerFontFamily
+from reportlab.platypus import (
+    BaseDocTemplate,
+    Frame,
+    HRFlowable,
+    KeepTogether,
+    ListFlowable,
+    ListItem,
+    PageBreak,
+    PageTemplate,
+    Paragraph,
+    Spacer,
+    Table,
+    TableStyle,
+)
+from reportlab.platypus.flowables import Flowable
+from reportlab.lib.styles import ParagraphStyle
+from reportlab.lib.enums import TA_CENTER, TA_LEFT
+from xml.sax.saxutils import escape as xml_escape
+
+
+INPUT_MD = Path("/mnt/data/260117-context-v6.md")
+OUTPUT_PDF = Path("/mnt/data/260117-context-v6.pdf")
+
+
+# -----------------------------
+# Fonts
+# -----------------------------
+
+
+def register_fonts() -> None:
+    """Register serif + sans families with bold/italic variants."""
+
+    roboto_dir = Path("/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF")
+    garamond_dir = Path("/usr/share/fonts/truetype/ebgaramond")
+
+    # Roboto
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont("Roboto", str(roboto_dir / "Roboto-Regular.ttf")))
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont("Roboto-Bold", str(roboto_dir / "Roboto-Bold.ttf")))
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont("Roboto-Italic", str(roboto_dir / "Roboto-Italic.ttf")))
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(
+        TTFont("Roboto-BoldItalic", str(roboto_dir / "Roboto-BoldItalic.ttf"))
+    )
+    registerFontFamily(
+        "Roboto",
+        normal="Roboto",
+        bold="Roboto-Bold",
+        italic="Roboto-Italic",
+        boldItalic="Roboto-BoldItalic",
+    )
+
+    # EB Garamond
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont("Garamond", str(garamond_dir / "EBGaramond12-Regular.ttf")))
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont("Garamond-Bold", str(garamond_dir / "EBGaramond12-Bold.ttf")))
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(
+        TTFont("Garamond-Italic", str(garamond_dir / "EBGaramond12-Italic.ttf"))
+    )
+    # Small caps variant is useful for subtle labels.
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont("Garamond-AllSC", str(garamond_dir / "EBGaramond12-AllSC.ttf")))
+    registerFontFamily(
+        "Garamond",
+        normal="Garamond",
+        bold="Garamond-Bold",
+        italic="Garamond-Italic",
+        boldItalic="Garamond-Bold",
+    )
+
+    # Monospace (for URLs if needed)
+    pdfmetrics.registerFont(
+        TTFont("DejaVuSansMono", "/usr/share/fonts/truetype/dejavu/DejaVuSansMono.ttf")
+    )
+
+
+# -----------------------------
+# Palette & layout
+# -----------------------------
+
+
+@dataclass(frozen=True)
+class Palette:
+    page_bg: colors.Color
+    text: colors.Color
+    muted: colors.Color
+    hairline: colors.Color
+    table_header_bg: colors.Color
+    table_row_alt: colors.Color
+    callout_bg: colors.Color
+    callout_border: colors.Color
+    link: colors.Color
+
+
+PALETTE = Palette(
+    page_bg=colors.HexColor("#F4F1EA"),
+    text=colors.HexColor("#1B1B1B"),
+    muted=colors.HexColor("#5A5A5A"),
+    hairline=colors.HexColor("#D6D0C5"),
+    table_header_bg=colors.HexColor("#F0ECE3"),
+    table_row_alt=colors.HexColor("#FBFAF7"),
+    callout_bg=colors.HexColor("#F7F4ED"),
+    callout_border=colors.HexColor("#D9D2C5"),
+    link=colors.HexColor("#1E4E8C"),
+)
+
+
+PAGE_W, PAGE_H = A4
+
+# A deliberately narrow reading column, centered.
+COLUMN_W = 360  # points (~5 inches)
+FRAME_X = (PAGE_W - COLUMN_W) / 2
+FRAME_Y = 72
+FRAME_H = PAGE_H - 2 * FRAME_Y
+
+
+# -----------------------------
+# Custom ornament
+# -----------------------------
+
+
+class TriOrnament(Flowable):
+    """Three small right-pointing triangles centered on the column."""
+
+    def __init__(self, width: float = 36, height: float = 10, color: colors.Color = PALETTE.text):
+        super().__init__()
+        self.width = width
+        self.height = height
+        self.color = color
+
+    def draw(self):
+        c = self.canv
+        c.saveState()
+        c.setFillColor(self.color)
+        c.setStrokeColor(self.color)
+        tri_w = self.width / 3.4
+        tri_h = self.height * 0.8
+        gap = tri_w * 0.15
+        start_x = (self.width - (3 * tri_w + 2 * gap)) / 2
+        y = (self.height - tri_h) / 2
+        for i in range(3):
+            x = start_x + i * (tri_w + gap)
+            # Triangle pointing right
+            p = c.beginPath()
+            p.moveTo(x, y)
+            p.lineTo(x, y + tri_h)
+            p.lineTo(x + tri_w, y + tri_h / 2)
+            p.close()
+            c.drawPath(p, stroke=0, fill=1)
+        c.restoreState()
+
+    def wrap(self, availWidth, availHeight):
+        return self.width, self.height
+
+
+# -----------------------------
+# Markdown -> Flowables
+# -----------------------------
+
+
+def _clean_whitespace(s: str) -> str:
+    # Collapse runs of whitespace but keep deliberate newlines as <br/>.
+    s = re.sub(r"[\t\r]+", " ", s)
+    s = re.sub(r" +", " ", s)
+    return s.strip()
+
+
+def rl_markup_from_node(node) -> str:
+    """Convert a BeautifulSoup node into ReportLab Paragraph markup."""
+    if isinstance(node, NavigableString):
+        return xml_escape(str(node))
+
+    if not isinstance(node, Tag):
+        return ""
+
+    name = node.name.lower()
+
+    if name in {"strong", "b"}:
+        return "<b>" + "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children) + "</b>"
+    if name in {"em", "i"}:
+        return "<i>" + "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children) + "</i>"
+    if name == "br":
+        return "<br/>"
+    if name == "code":
+        inner = "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children)
+        return f'<font face="DejaVuSansMono">{inner}</font>'
+    if name == "a":
+        href = node.get("href", "")
+        inner = "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children)
+        # Underline + link
+        href_esc = xml_escape(href, {"\"": "&quot;"})
+        return f'<link href="{href_esc}"><u>{inner}</u></link>'
+    if name in {"p", "span", "div"}:
+        return "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children)
+    if name == "sup":
+        inner = "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children)
+        return f"<super>{inner}</super>"
+    if name == "sub":
+        inner = "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children)
+        return f"<sub>{inner}</sub>"
+
+    # Default: recurse
+    return "".join(rl_markup_from_node(c) for c in node.children)
+
+
+def para_from_tag(tag: Tag, style: ParagraphStyle) -> Paragraph:
+    markup = rl_markup_from_node(tag)
+    markup = markup.replace("\n", " ")
+    markup = _clean_whitespace(markup)
+    return Paragraph(markup, style)
+
+
+def list_from_tag(tag: Tag, body_style: ParagraphStyle, ordered: bool) -> ListFlowable:
+    items: List[ListItem] = []
+    for li in tag.find_all("li", recursive=False):
+        # Some markdown converters wrap list item content in <p>.
+        li_children = [c for c in li.children if not (isinstance(c, NavigableString) and not str(c).strip())]
+        sub_flowables: List[Flowable] = []
+        for child in li_children:
+            if isinstance(child, Tag) and child.name.lower() == "p":
+                sub_flowables.append(para_from_tag(child, body_style))
+            elif isinstance(child, Tag) and child.name.lower() in {"ul", "ol"}:
+                sub_flowables.append(Spacer(1, 2))
+                sub_flowables.append(list_from_tag(child, body_style, ordered=child.name.lower() == "ol"))
+            elif isinstance(child, Tag):
+                # Treat as inline.
+                p = Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(rl_markup_from_node(child)), body_style)
+                sub_flowables.append(p)
+        if not sub_flowables:
+            sub_flowables = [Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(rl_markup_from_node(li)), body_style)]
+        items.append(ListItem(KeepTogether(sub_flowables), leftIndent=0))
+
+    bullet_type = "1" if ordered else "bullet"
+    lf = ListFlowable(
+        items,
+        bulletType=bullet_type,
+        start="1",
+        leftIndent=14,
+        bulletFontName=body_style.fontName,
+        bulletFontSize=body_style.fontSize,
+        bulletOffsetY=1,
+        bulletDedent=8,
+    )
+    return lf
+
+
+def _table_col_widths(text_matrix: List[List[str]], avail_width: float) -> List[float]:
+    """Heuristic column widths based on character lengths."""
+    n_cols = max(len(r) for r in text_matrix) if text_matrix else 1
+    # Pad
+    for r in text_matrix:
+        if len(r) < n_cols:
+            r.extend([""] * (n_cols - len(r)))
+
+    # Character-length heuristic (cap extremes)
+    col_lens = [0] * n_cols
+    for r in text_matrix:
+        for i, cell in enumerate(r):
+            l = len(re.sub(r"\s+", " ", cell.strip()))
+            col_lens[i] = max(col_lens[i], min(l, 80))
+    col_lens = [max(8, l) for l in col_lens]
+    total = sum(col_lens)
+    rel = [l / total for l in col_lens]
+
+    min_w = 52
+    max_w = avail_width * 0.62
+    widths = [max(min_w, min(max_w, avail_width * r)) for r in rel]
+    # Scale to fit exactly
+    s = sum(widths)
+    if s != 0:
+        widths = [w * (avail_width / s) for w in widths]
+
+    # If any width is below min due to scaling, re-balance once.
+    for _ in range(2):
+        deficit = 0.0
+        surplus = 0.0
+        for i, w in enumerate(widths):
+            if w < min_w:
+                deficit += min_w - w
+                widths[i] = min_w
+            elif w > min_w:
+                surplus += w - min_w
+        if deficit > 0 and surplus > 0:
+            # Reduce columns above min proportionally.
+            reducible = [max(0.0, w - min_w) for w in widths]
+            reducible_sum = sum(reducible)
+            if reducible_sum > 0:
+                for i in range(n_cols):
+                    if reducible[i] > 0:
+                        widths[i] -= deficit * (reducible[i] / reducible_sum)
+        s = sum(widths)
+        if abs(s - avail_width) > 0.5:
+            widths = [w * (avail_width / s) for w in widths]
+
+    return widths
+
+
+def table_from_tag(
+    table: Tag,
+    cell_style: ParagraphStyle,
+    header_style: ParagraphStyle,
+    avail_width: float,
+) -> Table:
+    rows: List[List[Flowable]] = []
+    plain: List[List[str]] = []
+    has_header = False
+    for tr in table.find_all("tr"):
+        row: List[Flowable] = []
+        plain_row: List[str] = []
+        ths = tr.find_all("th", recursive=False)
+        tds = tr.find_all("td", recursive=False)
+        cells = ths + tds
+        if ths:
+            has_header = True
+        for cell in cells:
+            txt_plain = cell.get_text(" ", strip=True)
+            plain_row.append(txt_plain)
+            st = header_style if cell.name.lower() == "th" else cell_style
+            row.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(rl_markup_from_node(cell)), st))
+        rows.append(row)
+        plain.append(plain_row)
+
+    n_cols = max(len(r) for r in rows) if rows else 1
+    for r in rows:
+        if len(r) < n_cols:
+            r.extend([Paragraph("", cell_style)] * (n_cols - len(r)))
+    for r in plain:
+        if len(r) < n_cols:
+            r.extend([""] * (n_cols - len(r)))
+
+    col_widths = _table_col_widths(plain, avail_width)
+
+    t = Table(
+        rows,
+        colWidths=col_widths,
+        hAlign="LEFT",
+        repeatRows=1 if has_header else 0,
+        splitByRow=1,
+    )
+
+    # Styling
+    style_cmds: List[Tuple] = [
+        ("VALIGN", (0, 0), (-1, -1), "TOP"),
+        ("LEFTPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 5),
+        ("RIGHTPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 5),
+        ("TOPPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 4),
+        ("BOTTOMPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 4),
+        ("LINEABOVE", (0, 0), (-1, 0), 0.6, PALETTE.hairline),
+        ("LINEBELOW", (0, 0), (-1, 0), 0.6, PALETTE.hairline),
+        ("LINEBELOW", (0, -1), (-1, -1), 0.4, PALETTE.hairline),
+        ("BOX", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 0.6, PALETTE.hairline),
+        ("INNERGRID", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 0.25, PALETTE.hairline),
+    ]
+    if has_header:
+        style_cmds.append(("BACKGROUND", (0, 0), (-1, 0), PALETTE.table_header_bg))
+    # Alternating row background (skip header)
+    start_row = 1 if has_header else 0
+    for r in range(start_row, len(rows)):
+        if (r - start_row) % 2 == 1:
+            style_cmds.append(("BACKGROUND", (0, r), (-1, r), PALETTE.table_row_alt))
+    t.setStyle(TableStyle(style_cmds))
+    return t
+
+
+def callout_from_blockquote(
+    bq: Tag,
+    body_style: ParagraphStyle,
+    list_style: ParagraphStyle,
+    avail_width: float,
+) -> Table:
+    """Render a blockquote as a subtle callout box."""
+    inner: List[Flowable] = []
+    for child in [c for c in bq.children if not (isinstance(c, NavigableString) and not str(c).strip())]:
+        if isinstance(child, Tag) and child.name.lower() == "p":
+            inner.append(para_from_tag(child, body_style))
+            inner.append(Spacer(1, 4))
+        elif isinstance(child, Tag) and child.name.lower() in {"ul", "ol"}:
+            inner.append(list_from_tag(child, list_style, ordered=child.name.lower() == "ol"))
+            inner.append(Spacer(1, 4))
+        else:
+            if isinstance(child, Tag):
+                inner.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(rl_markup_from_node(child)), body_style))
+                inner.append(Spacer(1, 4))
+    if inner and isinstance(inner[-1], Spacer):
+        inner.pop()
+    box = Table(
+        [[inner]],
+        colWidths=[avail_width],
+        hAlign="LEFT",
+    )
+    box.setStyle(
+        TableStyle(
+            [
+                ("BACKGROUND", (0, 0), (-1, -1), PALETTE.callout_bg),
+                ("BOX", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 0.7, PALETTE.callout_border),
+                ("LEFTPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 10),
+                ("RIGHTPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 10),
+                ("TOPPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 10),
+                ("BOTTOMPADDING", (0, 0), (-1, -1), 10),
+            ]
+        )
+    )
+    return box
+
+
+def build_story(md_text: str, styles: dict, frame_width: float) -> List[Flowable]:
+    html = markdown2.markdown(
+        md_text,
+        extras=[
+            "tables",
+            "fenced-code-blocks",
+            "strike",
+            "break-on-newline",
+        ],
+    )
+    soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")
+
+    story: List[Flowable] = []
+    seen_first_hr = False
+    cover_mode = True
+
+    for node in [c for c in soup.children if not (isinstance(c, NavigableString) and not str(c).strip())]:
+        if not isinstance(node, Tag):
+            continue
+        name = node.name.lower()
+
+        if name == "h1":
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 60))
+            story.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(node.get_text(" ", strip=True)), styles["title"]))
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 18))
+            continue
+
+        if cover_mode and name == "p":
+            # Cover metadata (centered).
+            story.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(rl_markup_from_node(node)), styles["meta"]))
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 6))
+            continue
+
+        if name == "hr":
+            if not seen_first_hr:
+                # First divider becomes a clean cover break.
+                story.append(Spacer(1, 18))
+                story.append(TriOrnament(width=40, height=10, color=PALETTE.hairline))
+                story.append(PageBreak())
+                seen_first_hr = True
+                cover_mode = False
+            else:
+                story.append(Spacer(1, 10))
+                story.append(HRFlowable(width="60%", thickness=0.6, color=PALETTE.hairline, spaceBefore=0, spaceAfter=0))
+                story.append(Spacer(1, 10))
+            continue
+
+        # After the first HR, treat remaining nodes as body.
+        if name == "h2":
+            story.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(node.get_text(" ", strip=True)), styles["h2"]))
+            continue
+        if name == "h3":
+            story.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(node.get_text(" ", strip=True)), styles["h3"]))
+            continue
+        if name == "h4":
+            story.append(Paragraph(_clean_whitespace(node.get_text(" ", strip=True)), styles["h4"]))
+            continue
+
+        if name == "p":
+            # If the paragraph is only whitespace, skip.
+            if not node.get_text(strip=True):
+                continue
+            story.append(para_from_tag(node, styles["body"]))
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 6))
+            continue
+
+        if name in {"ul", "ol"}:
+            story.append(list_from_tag(node, styles["body"], ordered=name == "ol"))
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 8))
+            continue
+
+        if name == "table":
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 6))
+            story.append(
+                table_from_tag(
+                    node,
+                    cell_style=styles["table_cell"],
+                    header_style=styles["table_header"],
+                    avail_width=frame_width,
+                )
+            )
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 12))
+            continue
+
+        if name == "blockquote":
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 6))
+            story.append(
+                callout_from_blockquote(
+                    node,
+                    body_style=styles["blockquote"],
+                    list_style=styles["blockquote_list"],
+                    avail_width=frame_width,
+                )
+            )
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 12))
+            continue
+
+        # Fallback: try to render as a paragraph.
+        txt = _clean_whitespace(node.get_text(" ", strip=True))
+        if txt:
+            story.append(Paragraph(xml_escape(txt), styles["body"]))
+            story.append(Spacer(1, 6))
+
+    # Clean trailing spacers
+    while story and isinstance(story[-1], Spacer):
+        story.pop()
+
+    return story
+
+
+# -----------------------------
+# Document
+# -----------------------------
+
+
+def make_styles() -> dict:
+    body = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="Body",
+        fontName="Roboto",
+        fontSize=9.2,
+        leading=12.6,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+        alignment=TA_LEFT,
+        spaceBefore=0,
+        spaceAfter=0,
+    )
+
+    title = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="Title",
+        fontName="Garamond",
+        fontSize=24,
+        leading=28,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+        alignment=TA_CENTER,
+        spaceAfter=0,
+    )
+
+    meta = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="Meta",
+        fontName="Roboto",
+        fontSize=9.2,
+        leading=13,
+        textColor=PALETTE.muted,
+        alignment=TA_CENTER,
+    )
+
+    h2 = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="H2",
+        fontName="Garamond-Bold",
+        fontSize=14.5,
+        leading=18,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+        spaceBefore=18,
+        spaceAfter=8,
+    )
+
+    h3 = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="H3",
+        fontName="Garamond",
+        fontSize=11.8,
+        leading=15,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+        spaceBefore=14,
+        spaceAfter=6,
+    )
+
+    h4 = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="H4",
+        fontName="Garamond-Italic",
+        fontSize=10.6,
+        leading=14,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+        spaceBefore=12,
+        spaceAfter=6,
+    )
+
+    table_header = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="TableHeader",
+        fontName="Roboto-Bold",
+        fontSize=8.4,
+        leading=10.4,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+    )
+    table_cell = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="TableCell",
+        fontName="Roboto",
+        fontSize=8.4,
+        leading=10.4,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+    )
+
+    blockquote = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="BlockQuote",
+        fontName="Roboto-Italic",
+        fontSize=8.7,
+        leading=12.0,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+    )
+    blockquote_list = ParagraphStyle(
+        name="BlockQuoteList",
+        fontName="Roboto",
+        fontSize=8.7,
+        leading=12.0,
+        textColor=PALETTE.text,
+    )
+
+    return {
+        "body": body,
+        "title": title,
+        "meta": meta,
+        "h2": h2,
+        "h3": h3,
+        "h4": h4,
+        "table_header": table_header,
+        "table_cell": table_cell,
+        "blockquote": blockquote,
+        "blockquote_list": blockquote_list,
+    }
+
+
+def _on_page(canvas, doc):
+    canvas.saveState()
+    # Background
+    canvas.setFillColor(PALETTE.page_bg)
+    canvas.rect(0, 0, PAGE_W, PAGE_H, stroke=0, fill=1)
+
+    # Subtle footer line
+    canvas.setStrokeColor(PALETTE.hairline)
+    canvas.setLineWidth(0.6)
+    x0 = FRAME_X
+    x1 = FRAME_X + COLUMN_W
+    y = 54
+    canvas.line(x0, y, x1, y)
+
+    # Page number
+    canvas.setFillColor(PALETTE.muted)
+    canvas.setFont("Roboto", 8)
+    canvas.drawCentredString(PAGE_W / 2, 36, str(doc.page))
+    canvas.restoreState()
+
+
+def build_pdf() -> Path:
+    register_fonts()
+    styles = make_styles()
+    md_text = INPUT_MD.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
+    story = build_story(md_text, styles, frame_width=COLUMN_W)
+
+    doc = BaseDocTemplate(
+        str(OUTPUT_PDF),
+        pagesize=A4,
+        leftMargin=0,
+        rightMargin=0,
+        topMargin=0,
+        bottomMargin=0,
+        title="ECAA Family ILR Application — Comprehensive Context v6",
+        author="",
+    )
+
+    frame = Frame(
+        FRAME_X,
+        FRAME_Y,
+        COLUMN_W,
+        FRAME_H,
+        leftPadding=0,
+        rightPadding=0,
+        topPadding=0,
+        bottomPadding=0,
+        showBoundary=0,
+    )
+
+    template = PageTemplate(id="main", frames=[frame], onPage=_on_page)
+    doc.addPageTemplates([template])
+    doc.build(story)
+    return OUTPUT_PDF
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    out = build_pdf()
+    print(f"Wrote: {out}")
+
*** End Patch
PATCH
Analyzed
Fixing patch issue—completing file

It looks like the patch ended prematurely due to the ellipsis inserted. That "from typing..." import was incomplete, which could result in a syntax error. I need to open the file and ensure the patch applies properly, filling in the file with the complete content to prevent any issues.

Reviewing script details

The script looks good overall, with features like narrow columns and page numbers included. However, there are missing elements, like the link color. Some font issues might arise with the italic and bold formatting for Garamond. Regarding the cover page, the title isn’t vertically centered, which could be adjusted.

Finalizing script review

The script seems to handle paragraph and meta tag formatting well, using <br/> for line breaks when there's a single paragraph. It inserts spacers and ornaments, though there might be too many small spaces between paragraphs. I'll run the script to see the PDF output and check for any spacing issues.

Evaluating missing features and layout issues in PDF rendering
python
bash -lc python /render_context_pdf.py
Analyzed
Investigating error with PageBreak

It seems the error relates to inserting a PageBreak inside a KeepTogether or within a table cell, which isn't allowed. The stack trace points to flowables.py where KeepTogether is involved. I’ll review the flowables setup, especially in list items or any callout boxes with tables, to find the root cause.

Refining PageBreak handling

It looks like PageBreak is possibly being treated as a regular flowable inside another Flowable, like KeepTogether, which isn't right. The error points to _FrameBreak.wrap, but PageBreak shouldn’t interact with wrap. I'll check the story’s flowables setup and ensure proper handling, especially around PageBreak.

Reevaluating PageBreak nesting

It looks like PageBreak may be mistakenly nested inside another flowable like KeepTogether or a table, which could trigger a wrap.
In the current structure, we only have one hr after the meta section that should be followed by the PageBreak.
I’ll need to check around where PageBreak might be improperly nested.

Looking into possible PageBreak nesting

I’m considering if a PageBreak might be incorrectly nested inside a list. Lists can have nested tags, like paragraphs or spacers, and list_from_tag.
I’m also thinking about how markdown horizontal rules (---) might be processed. They could turn into HTML hr tags, and that could be inside a blockquote or table cell. I’ll keep checking.

Investigating the _FrameBreak error inside KeepTogether

The error seems to be due to a _FrameBreak inside KeepTogether, which tries to call wrap on it. It’s likely caused by KeepTogether wrapping an inner flowable, such as _FrameBreak. The flowable might be coming from list_from_tag, which handles li_children. I’ll debug and modify the script to reproduce this issue.

Investigating issue with KeepTogether and _FrameBreak

KeepTogether seems to insert a _FrameBreak to manage large content, but this might conflict when trying to wrap. Normally, KeepTogether should handle frame breaks properly, so I’m considering bypassing it in ListItem since it’s optional. I’ll also check if ListItem can handle lists directly.

Exploring ListItem for flowables

I’m considering using ListItem(sub_flowables) in the code. From what I understand, ListItem is a Flowable that can contain flowables, including a list of them. I'll check the ReportLab documentation next to confirm how it handles lists within ListItem and verify its usage.

Troubleshooting PageBreak inside KeepTogether usage
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
from reportlab.platypus import ListItem
import inspect
print(ListItem)
print(inspect.signature(ListItem.__init__))
print(inspect.getsource(ListItem.__init__))
PY
Analyzed
Modifying list handling for flowables

I realized ListItem can accept either a single flowable or a list/tuple of them, converting it to a tuple. This means I can pass a list of flowables directly, removing the need for KeepTogether. I'll update list_from_tag to append ListItem(sub_flowables), skipping the KeepTogether step. I'll fix this in both the regular list and callout box sections.

Modifying list item handling for flowables
python
bash -lc apply_patch<<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /render_context_pdf.py
@@
-        items.append(ListItem(KeepTogether(sub_flowables), leftIndent=0))
+        # Avoid wrapping list items in KeepTogether: it can introduce internal frame
+        # breaks which ReportLab attempts to wrap, raising NotImplementedError.
+        items.append(ListItem(sub_flowables, leftIndent=0))
*** End Patch
PATCH
Analyzed
Fixing patch file path issue

It looks like apply_patch doesn't support absolute paths. Even though it says "Done," it might have applied the patch to a relative path instead. I need to use the file name without the leading slash (e.g., "render_context_pdf.py") to correctly patch the file from the current directory. I'll update the patch accordingly.

*** Update File: render_context_pdf.py
@@
-        items.append(ListItem(KeepTogether(sub_flowables), leftIndent=0))
+        # Avoid wrapping list items in KeepTogether: it can introduce internal frame
+        # breaks which ReportLab then tries to wrap, raising NotImplementedError.
+        items.append(ListItem(sub_flowables, leftIndent=0))
*** End Patch

Analyzed image
Adjusting ornament alignment

Page 1 looks good overall, but the triangles seem off-center. They’re meant to be centered, but due to the frame's left alignment, they appear on the column's left side. To fix this, I can modify the TriOrnament to center itself, maybe by adjusting the width or using a Table or Spacer. This should do the trick!

Fixing ornament positioning and adjusting page numbers

The triangles are currently aligned to the left of the column, due to fixed width, so they don’t center correctly on the page. To center them, I can set the TriOrnament’s width to match the available width. As for the page number, I’ll consider hiding it on the cover page, though it’s not essential for now. Analyzed image
Fixing missing arrow in "Urgency" line

The "5yr→10yr" arrow seems to have disappeared when rendering the page, possibly due to XML escape or font rendering issues. To avoid confusion, I'll replace the arrow with the ASCII "->", which is clearer and more likely to render across different systems. The user will likely appreciate clarity over aesthetics.

Addressing potential arrow rendering issue

It seems the arrow might not be showing due to font compatibility or how the PDF interprets it. Even though Roboto likely supports the arrow (U+2192), there could be issues with embedding the Unicode character. I'll replace the arrow with "->" to ensure clarity and avoid font rendering issues.

Handling arrow and check mark rendering issues

It looks like the arrow "→" might be missing due to font limitations, so it’s safer to use "->" instead. For the checkmark, since Roboto doesn’t support emojis, I’ll replace "✅" with a standard "✓", ensuring better consistency without losing meaning. Also, "PASSED WITH DISTINCTION" looks fine in bold.

Handling emoji rendering issues

Since emojis like "✅" and "⚠️" may not render, I'll convert them into simpler glyphs. For example, I'll replace "✅" with "✓" and "⚠️" with "WARNING:". This ensures everything appears correctly, especially in Roboto, as some emojis and special characters might not display properly. I'll preprocess the Markdown text to handle this.

Handling formatting issues with symbols and headings

There are some symbols like "→", "✅", and "⚠️" that might not render properly, so I'll replace them with alternatives like "->", "✓", and "IMPORTANT:". Additionally, there is an issue with the "Family Details" heading showing as "I." instead of "1.", which seems like an unintended transformation. I'll check and fix it.

Investigating Roman numeral issue with headings

It seems the "1." in the heading "1. Family Details" got replaced with a roman numeral "I." in the PDF, likely due to font styling. Garamond might make the "1" look too similar to "I" because of its serif and base design. It's tricky, but I think I can fix it.

Checking font issues with numeral 1 in Garamond

It looks like the digit "1" may be interpreted as the letter "I" due to the Garamond font, which could be using oldstyle numerals. This might explain why "1." in the heading turned into "I.". However, other numbers like "2." are fine, so it’s more about the font and not a markdown or HTML issue.

Considering font style and numeral appearance

I think the issue with "1" looking like "I" is due to the font's design, where the digit might appear like a capital I due to its shape and size in Garamond. It’s not a huge problem, but I could adjust the font style or size if needed. Garamond-Bold might help, but it’s not essential.

Reviewing alignment and table design

I'll check further pages for any issues, like line breaks or list formatting.

Fixing list rendering issue

Page 3 looks good, but the bullet list under "Requirements" uses hyphens instead of actual bullets. This could be because markdown2 might have misinterpreted the list as plain text rather than a proper <ul> due to being in the same paragraph. I need to check the conversion process from the markdown to ensure it's rendering correctly as a list.

Investigating markdown list rendering

The "Requirements" list is showing hyphens instead of bullet points, possibly because there’s a misunderstanding in how markdown2 handles line breaks with the "break-on-newline" setting. This might lead to markdown not recognizing the list properly. I’ll check the markdown file specifically around line 65 to verify the blank lines and ensure the list is structured correctly.

Debugging markdown list rendering issue
python
bash -lc sed -n '60,90p' /mnt/data/260117-context-v6.md
Analyzed
Investigating markdown2 list parsing

The issue might be that there’s no blank line between the "Requirements:" paragraph and the list. Typically in markdown, a list can follow a paragraph directly if separated by a newline. However, markdown2 might require a blank line, which could be causing the list to be treated as part of the paragraph with line breaks instead of a separate list.

Improving markdown parsing for lists and line breaks

To fix the issue, we could remove the “break-on-newline” setting, which is causing the problem. Markdown already uses two spaces at the end of lines to create breaks, so we likely no longer need this extra setting. This should allow lists to parse properly without affecting address lines, which use double spaces for line breaks.

Removing break-on-newline to fix list parsing

Removing the "break-on-newline" extra from markdown2 should help fix the list parsing issue, as it will better recognize bullet and ordered lists. Paragraphs with newlines but no double spaces will merge, which is fine. However, we need to check for any intentional line breaks in content like blockquotes.

Updating script to remove break-on-newline

In email content, lines start with ">", marking blockquotes. In markdown, blank lines separate paragraphs, so "break-on-newline" isn't needed and can be removed from markdown2. This will allow proper parsing of lists and render correct bullets in unordered lists, improving content presentation.

Testing list parsing with markdown2

I noticed there’s no blank line between "Three disjunctive pathways:" and the list. It might still parse due to numbering, but I’m not certain. I’ll test removing "break-on-newline" and see whether markdown2 can handle lists even with no blank line. Let's run a quick test using container.exec to check.

Testing markdown2 behavior with line breaks
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
import markdown2, pathlib
text = pathlib.Path('/mnt/data/260117-context-v6.md').read_text('utf-8')
html1 = markdown2.markdown(text, extras=['tables','fenced-code-blocks','strike','break-on-newline'])
html2 = markdown2.markdown(text, extras=['tables','fenced-code-blocks','strike'])
# find snippet around 'Requirements:'
import re
for label, html in [('with_break', html1), ('no_break', html2)]:
    m = re.search(r'Requirements', html)
    print('\n', label)
    print(html[m.start()-200:m.start()+600])
PY
Analyzed
Fixing list parsing manually

So, it seems markdown2 still doesn’t parse the requirements list as a proper <ul> even without break-on-newline. It stays in the <p> with hyphens as plain text. I think we might need to detect paragraphs that start with "- " and convert them to list items ourselves since markdown2 likely wants a blank line. Let’s handle that.

Understanding list formatting behavior

Okay, so the newline was replaced with a space, but the conversion process might still be preserving certain characters like "\n". We previously used the break-on-newline extra to allow line breaks, which helped with readability but not list formatting. We need a proper solution to parse the list as actual list items.

Handling pseudo-lists and special formatting

It seems I need to convert "pseudo-lists" within paragraphs into actual lists, especially in sections like "Additional conditions satisfied" with hyphen bullets and check marks. There's also "Decision framework:" which might already parse, but I'll double-check the surrounding lines and ensure proper formatting. That should help.

Creating proper list formatting from paragraphs

I can split paragraphs where bullet points start appearing, like in "Requirements:"—treating the first line separately as a heading or label. Then I can create a list from the subsequent lines, ensuring the content is neat. For now, the label and bullets are together in one paragraph, but that could be cleaned up for readability.

Improving paragraph handling for lists and line breaks

I can enhance the algorithm by directly handling paragraphs with line breaks while preserving inline elements. The idea is to replace line breaks with \n markers, while still distinguishing paragraphs from pseudo-lists. When bullet points appear, I’ll detect them and convert them into list items, keeping text with actual line breaks as paragraphs.

Optimizing requirements paragraph handling

For the "Requirements" paragraph, I’ll use node.get_text("\n", strip=False) to capture the text, identifying the label like "Requirements:". Then, I can check for lines that start with "- " or "1. " to detect list items. For this pattern, I'll treat the first line as a paragraph, using markup conversion for it, while treating subsequent lines as a list.

Preserving markup within list items

I’m thinking of an approach to preserve markup like bold text inside list items. For paragraphs, I can split them into segments based on <br> tags. Then I can convert each segment back to markup, preserving bold/italic and links. For list items, I'll detect bullet or numbered points and handle accordingly, ensuring we don’t lose important formatting like "Deadline:". I'll clean up unnecessary whitespace and ensure everything stays formatted neatly.

Handling list items and parsing pseudo-lists

I’m focusing on detecting whether each segment of a paragraph should be treated as a list item or paragraph, based on its starting pattern. Bullet items will be identified by marks like "- " or "•", while ordered items by numbers like "1.". For each, I'll clean up the markup, removing unnecessary markers, and will then construct the list using ListFlowable. I'll also ensure that any bold text is preserved by the Paragraph component.

Adjusting paragraphs and emoji replacements

I need to handle paragraph segments carefully: if they're not part of a list, we keep them as paragraphs and preserve <br/> tags. For meta or address-style paragraphs, I'll make sure to retrofit the whitespace cleaning process so <br/> stays intact. Additionally, before passing text for markdown conversion, I’ll replace certain characters like arrows and emojis properly to prevent rendering issues.

Fixing bullet list rendering issues

Investigating missing bullet symbol issue

The "1" instead of a bullet could mean the bulletType is being processed as an ordered list, or the bullet symbol isn't rendering correctly. Even though Roboto should support the bullet "•" symbol, I’ll double-check if it's missing in the font or replaced by a placeholder.

Investigating ListFlowable start parameter effect

I’m considering that the issue with the bullet showing as "1" might be because the start="1" value is affecting the bullet output. The start parameter could be interpreted as the bullet character if the bulletType is set to "bullet" instead of "1". I’ll check the documentation for confirmation.

Refining start parameter handling for bullets

It seems the start parameter might be setting the bullet character itself when it's used with a bullet list, possibly overriding it to "1" if improperly set. I'll adjust it: for ordered lists, "start" is a number, but for bullet lists, "start" should likely be None (or omitted) to avoid problems like showing "1" instead of a bullet. I'll confirm in the documentation.

Inspecting ListFlowable constructor and parameters
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
from reportlab.platypus import ListFlowable
import inspect
print(inspect.signature(ListFlowable.__init__))
print('---')
print(inspect.getsource(ListFlowable.__init__).splitlines()[:60])
PY
Analyzed
Fixing start parameter for bullets in ListFlowable

It looks like passing start="1" for bullet lists replaces the desired bullet character with "1". To fix this, I'll set start=None for bullet lists, and use "1" only for ordered lists. I'll also adjust pseudo-list detection in the "Benchmarks" list to properly convert to bullets.

Finding missing "UK birth certificate" in HTML output
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
import markdown2, pathlib, re
text = pathlib.Path('/mnt/data/260117-context-v6.md').read_text('utf-8')
html = markdown2.markdown(text, extras=['tables','fenced-code-blocks','strike','break-on-newline'])
# Find the 'UK birth certificate' occurrence in HTML
idx = html.find('UK birth certificate')
print('idx', idx)
print(html[idx-200:idx+200])
PY
Analyzed
Investigating arrow character issue and solution

The issue seems to lie in how the arrow "←" is handled. It might not be supported in certain fonts, causing it to be dropped or not rendered. If we replace it with "<-", it could be processed correctly after escaping it as "<-". This should preserve the content and prevent any unintended removal.

Investigating missing text and possible line wrapping

The "UK birth certificate" is likely missing due to line wrapping issues. In the bullet list, the text might have been pushed to a previous line, causing it to be hidden. Since it's not visible on page 12, it seems like the text before the arrow—including "[x] UK birth certificate"—was dropped, leaving only "required per lawyer".

Exploring the role of the arrow character

It seems the issue might be due to the arrow character potentially being interpreted incorrectly. Our parser should handle the markup with the arrow included, but maybe the arrow causes an issue in the encoding or with the font rendering in ReportLab. The "UK birth certificate" should display correctly since it's ASCII, but the arrow seems to be the culprit.

Investigating text display issue

The missing "UK birth certificate" could be caused by present font issues or text wrapping. If the arrow isn't supported, it might affect the whole line, but it’s unlikely to cause earlier text loss. Maybe the issue is with list item indentation, causing part of the text to be off-page, though the rest displays fine.

Exploring list indentation issue and arrow character handling

I think the bullet number ‘1’ might be affecting text alignment because of the left and bullet indentation settings. The bullet itself could be taking space, and the arrow character might get misinterpreted or treated as a delimiter by the PDF handling tool. To confirm, I’ll try creating a simple PDF snippet to test this theory.

Investigating missing arrow character in PDF rendering
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
from reportlab.pdfbase import pdfmetrics
from reportlab.pdfbase.ttfonts import TTFont
from reportlab.pdfbase.pdfmetrics import registerFontFamily
from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, Paragraph
from reportlab.lib.styles import ParagraphStyle
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import A4
from reportlab.lib import colors

pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Regular.ttf'))
pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto-Bold','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Bold.ttf'))
registerFontFamily('Roboto', normal='Roboto', bold='Roboto-Bold')

style = ParagraphStyle('body', fontName='Roboto', fontSize=12, leading=14, textColor=colors.black)

doc = SimpleDocTemplate('/mnt/data/test_arrow.pdf', pagesize=A4)
text = '[x] UK birth certificate ← <b>required per lawyer</b>'
doc.build([Paragraph(text, style)])
print('wrote')
PY
Analyzed
Checking if script truncation caused by apply_patch

It seems like the script might've been truncated due to apply_patch. I used pdfmetrics.register..., but there may be an error from the ellipsis. However, the script printed "wrote," so maybe it succeeded. To confirm, I'll check the test PDF output in /mnt/data/test_arrow.pdf.

Testing with arrow character

In this test, we didn’t include the arrow "←"; we just typed "[x] UK birth certificate required per lawyer." So, the arrow wasn’t the issue. Now, let's create a new test specifically with the arrow character to see what happens.

Generating PDF with arrows and text
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
from reportlab.pdfbase import pdfmetrics
from reportlab.pdfbase.ttfonts import TTFont
from reportlab.pdfbase.pdfmetrics import registerFontFamily
from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, Paragraph, Spacer
from reportlab.lib.styles import ParagraphStyle
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import A4

pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Regular.ttf'))
pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto-Bold','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Bold.ttf'))
registerFontFamily('Roboto', normal='Roboto', bold='Roboto-Bold')

style = ParagraphStyle('b', fontName='Roboto', fontSize=14)

doc = SimpleDocTemplate('/mnt/data/test_arrow2.pdf', pagesize=A4)
story = [Paragraph('[x] UK birth certificate ← <b>required per lawyer</b>', style), Spacer(1,20),
         Paragraph('Arrow right: 5yr→10yr', style)]
doc.build(story)
print('wrote')
PY
Analyzed
Checking output file

So, the script seems to have used ellipsis, but it still printed "wrote." Hmm, maybe the ellipsis wasn't included in the path after all. Let’s open the file "/mnt/data/test_arrow2.pdf" to check the output and see what’s actually there. I’ll list it to confirm.

Investigating missing arrow symbols in PDF rendering
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
from reportlab.pdfbase import pdfmetrics
from reportlab.pdfbase.ttfonts import TTFont
from reportlab.pdfbase.pdfmetrics import registerFontFamily
from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, ListFlowable, ListItem, Paragraph
from reportlab.lib.styles import ParagraphStyle
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import A4

pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Regular.ttf'))
pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto-Bold','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Bold.ttf'))
registerFontFamily('Roboto', normal='Roboto', bold='Roboto-Bold', italic='Roboto', boldItalic='Roboto-Bold')

style = ParagraphStyle('body', fontName='Roboto', fontSize=11, leading=14)
items = [ListItem(Paragraph('[x] UK birth certificate ← <b>required per lawyer</b>', style))]
lf = ListFlowable(items, bulletType='bullet', start='1', leftIndent=20, bulletDedent=10, bulletFontName='Roboto', bulletFontSize=11)
SimpleDocTemplate('/mnt/data/test_list_bug.pdf', pagesize=A4).build([lf])
print('wrote')
PY
Analyzed
Checking test list with ellipsis

I used ellipsis again, but the script worked, even though I’m unsure why. Something feels off, though. Let’s open the test list and check the file at /mnt/data/test_listarrow.pdf to better understand the results and see if there’s anything unexpected going on with the ellipsis.

Finding test list file
python
bash -lc ls -1 /mnt/data | grep test_listarrow || true
Analyzed
Running the script properly

Hmm, the file doesn't seem to be there. Maybe the script failed due to something with the ellipsis, even though it printed "wrote". That output could have come from a previous run of the script. I need to rerun it properly and make sure the script writes fully this time.

Generating PDF with styled lists
python
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
from reportlab.pdfbase import pdfmetrics
from reportlab.pdfbase.ttfonts import TTFont
from reportlab.pdfbase.pdfmetrics import registerFontFamily
from reportlab.platypus import SimpleDocTemplate, ListFlowable, ListItem, Paragraph, Spacer
from reportlab.lib.styles import ParagraphStyle
from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import A4

pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Regular.ttf'))
pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto-Bold','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Bold.ttf'))
pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto-Italic','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-Italic.ttf'))
pdfmetrics.registerFont(TTFont('Roboto-BoldItalic','/usr/share/fonts/truetype/roboto/unhinted/RobotoTTF/Roboto-BoldItalic.ttf'))
registerFontFamily('Roboto', normal='Roboto', bold='Roboto-Bold', italic='Roboto-Italic', boldItalic='Roboto-BoldItalic')

style = ParagraphStyle('body', fontName='Roboto', fontSize=12, leading=15)

items = [
    ListItem([Paragraph('[x] UK birth certificate ← <b>required per lawyer</b>', style)]),
    ListItem([Paragraph('[x] Another item', style)])
]

lst = ListFlowable(items, bulletType='bullet', start='1', leftIndent=20, bulletDedent=10, bulletFontName='Roboto', bulletFontSize=12)

story = [Paragraph('List with start="1" on bulletType="bullet":', style), Spacer(1,12), lst]

SimpleDocTemplate('/mnt/data/test_list_start1.pdf', pagesize=A4).build(story)

items2=[
    ListItem([Paragraph('[x] UK birth certificate ← <b>required per lawyer</b>', style)]),
    ListItem([Paragraph('[x] Another item', style)])
]

lst2=ListFlowable(items2, bulletType='bullet', start=None, leftIndent=20, bulletDedent=10, bulletFontName='Roboto', bulletFontSize=12)

story2=[Paragraph('List with start=None on bulletType="bullet":', style), Spacer(1,12), lst2]
SimpleDocTemplate('/mnt/data/test_list_start_none.pdf', pagesize=A4).build(story2)

print('done')
PY
Analyzed
Thought for 62m 30s

Done
